pattern: A three-way split is beautiful exactly when one of its middle boundaries is forced to repeat the run of values that came just before it, which turns into a longest-common-prefix question between two suffixes of the same array.
hints: Fix the first cut point i; whether the first piece is a prefix of the second piece only depends on i and the length of the second piece, not on where the second cut lands, as long as the second piece is long enough.

Whether the second piece is a prefix of the third piece is really asking how many positions the suffix starting right after the first cut and the suffix starting right after the second cut agree on -- a longest-common-prefix-between-two-suffixes question.

You can answer 'how far do two suffixes agree' for every possible second cut in one pass per first cut by running a Z-function on the suffix that starts at the first cut, then reading off the shared-prefix length at the offset where the second cut would begin.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two beautiful splits from the same first cut
statement example, strictly increasing values give zero splits
shortest possible array, too small for any split
smallest length that admits a split, all values equal
every value identical, so almost every split qualifies and the counting formula must not double count
strictly increasing, longer than the anchor example, still zero splits
an alternating pattern that only partially repeats
a longer uniform array using the minimum allowed value, to exercise the performance-sensitive path at scale

stress.description: arrays biased toward repeated values (small alphabet) so beautiful splits are common, up to the maximum length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 5000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

complexity.time: O(n^2) -- an O(n) Z-function pass for each of the n possible first cuts
complexity.space: O(n) for the Z-function array reused per first cut
